The JSON-RPC interface provides methods for generating addresses, creating/sending transactions, retrieving balances and much more. The full specification is available here:
The following methods are particularly useful when interacting with the network:
: Retrieve transaction details using a transaction hash.
: Fetch transaction hashes associated with a given address.
: Returns the current head block of the node.
: Returns the block at the specified height
: Get raw transaction details by decoding a serialized transaction.
: Returns a boolean specifying if the node has established consensus with the network.
: Create a signed transaction and returns a hex-encoded representation without broadcasting it. All
RPC methods also have an equivalent
version in order to create and broadcast the transaction one-go.
: Sends the given serialized and signed transaction to the network.

Nimiq has transitioned from a PoW system to a PoS system. For those needing access to historical transaction data before the PoS genesis block, a read-only database of the PoW chain is available and can be queried using the
Pre-genesis transactions use a different format compared to PoS transactions. Instructions on how to handle and query these pre-genesis transactions will be provided in a future update.
Getting Started with the PoS Node

This section focuses on how to set up and configure your PoS node. The most important configuration settings are outlined below, but it is strongly recommended to review all the sections and apply them based on your requirements. Currently, two methods are supported for running a node: via Docker or by compiling from source code.

: Generates a Nimiq address and corresponding key pair. Outputs the user-friendly address, raw address, public key and private key.
language-bash shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight

cargo run --release --bin nimiq-address -- --help

: Signs a transaction using key parameters such as sender address, recipient address, value and fee. Outputs the signed transaction in hexadecimal format.

cargo run --release --bin nimiq-signtx -- --help
cargo run --release --bin nimiq-signtx -- --secret-key <secret_key> --from <sender_address> --to <recipient_address> --value <amount> --fee <fee> --validity-start-height <start_height> --network <network_id>
: A CLI RPC client that can query all the supported JSON-RPC methods.

cargo run --release --bin nimiq-rpc -- --help
cargo run --release --bin nimiq-rpc -- -u URL -U username -P password
After the next macro block (end of every batch)

validity window, meaning that a transaction is valid to be included in a block within that timeframe after the transaction's validity start height. If a transaction is not included within this window, it becomes invalid and will be rejected by the network.

Transactions can fail for multiple reasons, such as missing information or invalid parameters. Before a transaction is added to the blockchain, it must meet all the conditions. If any of the following errors occur, the transaction will be rejected by the network and not be included in a block:
: The sender address is missing from the transaction.
: The recipient address is missing. A valid recipient must be provided.
: The transaction value (amount to be transferred) is missing.
: The transaction’s validity start height, which defines from when the transaction becomes valid, is not set.
: The transaction’s network ID is missing. The ID identifies which network the transaction is intended, whether Testnet or Mainnet.
Once a valid transaction is included in a
(blocks containing user-generated transactions), finality is achieved through

. Macro blocks are produced after 59 micro blocks (~1 minute) using the Tendermint consensus algorithm. Once a macro block is added to the chain, all the transactions from the previous 59 micro blocks are finalized and have become irreversible.

Running the nimiq-client — From Source Code (Ubuntu)

This guide outlines the key steps and requirements for integrators, such as exchanges, who want to set up and operate a Proof-of-Stake (PoS) node for the Nimiq blockchain. It provides detailed information on node types, hardware requirements and configuration options to facilitate an efficient integration.
The transition from PoW to PoS is complete, and this section has been archived.
Mempool methods are only available when the node has a mempool.
